name: Amman
history: Amman has been inhabited since prehistoric times and was known as Philadelphia during the Hellenistic and Roman periods, when it became an important city in the Decapolis. The city declined after the Islamic conquest in the 7th century but experienced a modest revival under the Umayyads before falling into relative obscurity for centuries. In the early 20th century, Amman was chosen as the capital of the Emirate of Transjordan and grew rapidly, especially after Jordan's independence in 1946. The city has since expanded from a small town of a few thousand to a sprawling modern metropolis, though it retains reminders of its ancient past, including the impressive Roman Theatre and Citadel.
culturalNotes: Amman is a vibrant, cosmopolitan Arab city that has absorbed influences from its long history while maintaining strong Islamic and Levantine traditions. The city is known for its warm hospitality, bustling souks, contemporary art scene, and excellent restaurants that blend traditional Jordanian cuisine with modern innovation. Family and community are central to Amman's character, and visitors will notice the blend of traditional values with the progressive attitudes of a youthful, educated population.
bestTimeToVisit: October to April when temperatures are moderate and ideal for exploring the capital's museums and Roman ruins.
languageNotes: Arabic is the official language, and most residents speak Levantine Arabic with the distinctive Jordanian accent, though Modern Standard Arabic is used in formal settings. Useful phrases include "As-salamu alaikum" (peace be upon you) for greeting, "Shukran" (thank you), and "Inshallah" (God willing), which are woven into everyday conversation.

places.name: Roman Theater
places.description: A remarkably well-preserved 2nd-century Roman amphitheater carved into a hillside in downtown Amman, capable of seating 6,000 spectators. It remains one of the most iconic symbols of Amman's ancient Greco-Roman heritage and offers a tangible window into the city's classical past.

activities.detail: Climb the original Roman stone steps to experience the tiered seating arrangement and imagine how audiences of 6,000 gathered here nearly 2,000 years ago.

activities.text: Visit the Museum of Popular Traditions
activities.detail: Browse traditional Jordanian clothing, crafts, and household items displayed in the vaulted chambers beneath the theater stage.
Check the schedule for classical concerts, theatrical performances, or cultural events held during summer months on the original stage.
Ascend to the uppermost rows for panoramic views of downtown Amman and excellent vantage points for sunset photography of the theater's architecture.
Stand at various points in the arena to test the remarkable ancient acoustics that allow sound to carry clearly from the stage to the back rows.
Walk to the smaller Roman Odeon just north of the main theater to see another well-preserved 2nd-century performance venue.

food.detail: A traditional Jordanian dish of lamb cooked in yogurt sauce with pine nuts and served over flatbread, commonly found in restaurants near the theater.
Crispy chickpea fritters served in pita bread with hummus and fresh vegetables from vendors in the surrounding downtown Amman streets.
A Levantine salad of mixed greens, tomatoes, cucumbers, and radishes topped with sumac and crispy pita chips, typical of local cafés nearby.
A selection of small dishes including hummus, baba ganoush, tabbouleh, and pickled vegetables perfect for sharing at traditional restaurants in the area.
Traditional cardamom-spiced coffee paired with date or walnut-filled shortbread cookies, offered at cafés throughout downtown near the theater.

culture.detail: Wear modest clothing with covered shoulders and knees, especially if visiting during Islamic holidays or prayer times, as the area remains culturally significant.

culture.text: Visit Early Morning or Late Afternoon
culture.detail: The theater offers minimal shade, so plan your visit during cooler hours and bring water and sunscreen to stay comfortable while exploring the stone seating.
Hiring a guide at the entrance provides deeper historical context about the Greco-Roman period and Amman's ancient identity as Philadelphia.
Ask permission before photographing people or taking flash photos near ongoing performances or special events held at the theater.

places.name: Amman Citadel
places.description: Perched on Jebel Al-Qala'a hill overlooking the city, this archaeological site contains ruins spanning from the Neolithic period through the Islamic era, including a Byzantine church and Umayyad palace. The panoramic views of Amman combined with layers of historical significance make it essential for understanding the region's cultural continuity.

activities.detail: Walk through the remains of this 8th-century Islamic palace to see its distinctive arches, carved stonework, and the central courtyard that once hosted elite gatherings.
Examine the mosaic floors and architectural fragments of this 6th-century church, one of the earliest Christian structures in the region.
Stand on the hilltop to capture sweeping vistas of modern Amman's white-stone buildings and the surrounding valleys from the highest vantage point in the city.
Browse the on-site museum displaying artifacts from the Neolithic, Bronze Age, and Islamic periods, including pottery, sculptures, and inscriptions.
Trace the ancient settlement patterns visible in the terraced landscape that shows evidence of some of the region's earliest human habitation.
Inspect the Ammonite defensive tower from the Iron Age, representing the citadel's strategic military importance across millennia.

food.detail: This traditional Jordanian dish of lamb served in a yogurt-based sauce with pine nuts and flatbread pairs perfectly with the historical experience and is widely available at nearby cafés.
Fresh chickpea dip drizzled with olive oil and eaten with warm pita bread is a light, satisfying snack while exploring the ruins.
Deep-fried chickpea patties served with tahini sauce offer a quick, authentic vegetarian option at vendors near the citadel entrance.
Sip strong, cardamom-infused coffee with fresh dates at a nearby café to experience traditional Jordanian hospitality and take a rest.
This slow-roasted meat dish cooked in underground ovens represents local culinary heritage and can be found at restaurants adjacent to the archaeological site.

culture.detail: Wear modest clothing covering shoulders and knees out of respect for the sacred religious sites, particularly the Byzantine Church area.
Arrive early morning or late afternoon to avoid midday heat and enjoy better lighting for both photography and comfortable exploration of open ruins.
A knowledgeable guide from Amman can provide invaluable context about the overlapping civilizations and point out details that might otherwise go unnoticed.
Always ask permission before photographing locals and be mindful that some areas may have restrictions; religious sites especially warrant sensitivity.

places.name: Rainbow Street
places.description: A vibrant pedestrian thoroughfare in the Jabal Amman neighborhood painted with rainbow-colored steps and lined with trendy cafes, galleries, restaurants, and boutiques. It captures contemporary Amman culture and offers the best venue for experiencing the city's modern creative scene and local hospitality.

activities.detail: Stroll through the iconic pastel-painted staircase that gives the street its name, perfect for photos and people-watching.
Visit small art galleries and studios showcasing contemporary Jordanian artists, paintings, and crafts along the street.
Explore local fashion boutiques and vintage shops selling handmade jewelry, clothing, and artisan goods unique to Amman's creative community.
Tour the nearby museum housed in a historic villa featuring Palestinian and Jordanian cultural artifacts and contemporary exhibitions.
Enjoy traditional Arabic coffee or freshly squeezed juices while sitting on terraces overlooking Amman's hillside neighborhoods.
Check for evening performances by local musicians at cafes and galleries, especially on weekends when the street becomes livelier.

food.detail: Seek out street vendors or small eateries serving freshly made hummus topped with olive oil and warm falafel, staples of Jordanian street food.
Experience this iconic Jordanian dish of lamb cooked in yogurt sauce served with rice, offered at several traditional restaurants near the street.
Order vibrant pomegranate juice from juice stands, a specialty in Amman and refreshing given the street's warm afternoon atmosphere.
Enjoy thin bread topped with zaatar herb mix and olive oil, sold at bakeries and cafes throughout Rainbow Street.
Indulge in this sweet pastry dessert with cheese and pistachios, commonly served at cafes as an evening treat with strong Arabic coffee.

culture.detail: Come in late afternoon through evening when the street becomes most lively with people, musicians, and the cooler weather makes walking comfortable.
While casual Western clothing is acceptable on Rainbow Street, modest dress (avoiding very short shorts or revealing tops) shows respect for local culture.
Friendly negotiation over prices is expected at boutiques and small shops, and vendors appreciate when visitors engage in this traditional social custom.
Always ask permission before photographing locals or inside shops and galleries, as many artists and business owners appreciate the courtesy.

places.name: Jordan Museum
places.description: The national museum housed in a striking modern building showcasing Jordan's archaeological treasures from prehistoric times to the modern era, including artifacts from Petra and Dead Sea Scrolls. It provides comprehensive context for understanding the country's extraordinary historical significance.

activities.text: Explore the Dead Sea Scrolls Exhibition
activities.detail: View rare fragments of ancient biblical manuscripts discovered near the Dead Sea, displayed in a climate-controlled gallery that preserves these 2,000-year-old texts.
Study intricate stone vessels, pottery, and architectural fragments from the Nabataean city of Petra to understand the craftsmanship of this UNESCO World Heritage site.
Examine tools, weapons, and skeletal remains spanning from the Paleolithic to Neolithic periods, illustrating human settlement in the Levant region.

activities.text: Examine the Roman and Byzantine Artifacts
activities.detail: Discover mosaics, coins, glass vessels, and religious objects from when Jordan was part of the Roman and Byzantine empires.
Browse traditional clothing, jewelry, weapons, and household items that showcase Bedouin and Arab cultural heritage from recent centuries.

activities.text: Attend a Museum Lecture or Workshop
activities.detail: Check for scheduled talks by archaeologists and historians who provide deeper context on Jordan's historical periods and ongoing excavations.

food.detail: A traditional Jordanian dish of lamb cooked in a yogurt-based sauce served with rice or bread, commonly enjoyed by locals near cultural venues.
Crispy chickpea fritters and creamy chickpea paste, staple Levantine foods readily available at cafés near the museum.
Slow-cooked meat and vegetables buried underground, a Bedouin specialty that reflects Jordan's desert heritage and can be found at nearby restaurants.
A sweet pastry dessert made with shredded phyllo, cheese, and syrup, perfect for a post-museum treat at local bakeries.
Strong black tea infused with fresh mint leaves, the customary beverage served throughout Jordan for hospitality and refreshment.

culture.detail: Wear respectful clothing covering shoulders and knees, particularly when viewing religious artifacts and Dead Sea Scrolls exhibits.
Plan at least 2–3 hours to properly read artifact descriptions and historical context panels, as the museum prioritizes detailed interpretation over quick tours.
A simple 'As-salamu alaikum' (peace be upon you) is appreciated when entering exhibits, reflecting local courtesy customs.
Some sections, particularly the Dead Sea Scrolls gallery, prohibit photography to protect fragile artifacts, so check signage before taking pictures.

places.name: Darat Al-Funun
places.description: An independent art institution and cultural center housed in a beautifully restored historic building in Jabal Amman, hosting contemporary art exhibitions, performances, and cultural events. It represents Amman's thriving modern artistic community and offers curated cultural experiences beyond typical tourism.

activities.detail: Explore rotating exhibitions by emerging and established Arab and international artists in the institution's main gallery spaces.
Catch live music, theater, dance, or poetry readings held regularly in the courtyard or interior performance spaces during evenings and weekends.
Appreciate the carefully restored Ottoman-era mansion with its original stone arches, courtyards, and period details that blend heritage with modern curation.
Relax in the courtyard café serving coffee, light snacks, and refreshments in a tranquil setting surrounded by artistic ambiance.
Walk the surrounding historic district with its tree-lined streets, independent galleries, vintage shops, and restored villas reflecting Amman's cultural heritage.

activities.text: Participate in Artist Talks and Workshops
activities.detail: Join curator-led discussions, artist meet-and-greets, or hands-on creative workshops offered alongside major exhibitions.

food.detail: Flattened dough topped with a blend of dried oregano, sumac, and sesame oil, often served warm at the café as a light traditional snack.

food.text: Hummus with Olive Oil and Pine Nuts
food.detail: Creamy chickpea purée drizzled with premium local olive oil and garnished with toasted pine nuts, exemplifying Levantine simplicity.
Strong, cardamom-infused coffee served in small cups alongside fresh or dried dates, following traditional Jordanian hospitality customs.
Crispy fried chickpea patties wrapped in pita bread with tahini sauce and fresh vegetables, a beloved quick lunch option in the area.
Freshly squeezed citrus, pomegranate, or mixed fruit juices available at nearby neighborhood vendors, offering vibrant refreshment.

culture.detail: Galleries are often contemplative spaces where visitors should speak softly and avoid photography unless explicitly permitted to preserve the viewing experience.
While Amman is relatively liberal, wearing respectful clothing—avoiding very short shorts or sheer fabrics—is appreciated at cultural performances and gatherings.
Popular performances and exhibition openings draw crowds quickly; arriving 15–30 minutes early ensures good seating and a less rushed experience.
Jordanian artists and curators welcome thoughtful conversation about their work; showing sincere interest in their creative vision enriches the cultural exchange.

places.name: Ajloun Castle
places.description: A beautifully preserved 12th-century Islamic fortress perched on a hilltop with commanding views of the Jordan Valley, built to control trade routes and resist Crusader invasions. Located an hour north of Amman, it offers both historical significance and scenic mountain landscapes.

activities.detail: Walk through the four-story tower, climb the stone staircases, and examine the arrow slits, cisterns, and garrison chambers that reveal medieval military architecture.
Stand on the castle ramparts to gaze across the verdant Jordan Valley, the Dead Sea, and Palestinian territories with clarity on clear days.
Trek through the pine and oak forests around the hilltop fortress, enjoying cooler mountain air and glimpses of traditional Ajloun villages.
Browse artifacts including ceramics, coins, and weapons from the Ayyubid period that illustrate the castle's strategic and daily life.
Capture dramatic sunset shots of the fortress silhouetted against the valley, or sunrise mist rising from the Jordan Valley below.
Several nearby farms grow za'atar, thyme, and sage endemic to the Ajloun region; ask guides about picking or purchasing fresh herbs.

food.detail: A legendary Jordanian dish of lamb cooked in yogurt sauce served over flatbread and topped with pine nuts, best enjoyed at family restaurants in Ajloun town.
Fresh freshwater trout caught from local streams and grilled simply with lemon and herbs, reflecting the region's cool water sources.
Locally-harvested wild thyme mixed with olive oil and sumac spread on warm pita, a signature snack of the Ajloun highlands.
Tender vine leaves rolled with spiced rice and meat, common in Ajloun villages and often served warm with yogurt.
Thick creamy labne cheese drizzled with Ajloun-produced wildflower honey, representing the region's pastoral dairy tradition.

culture.detail: Ajloun sits at 800+ meters elevation; bring layers even in summer, as mornings and evenings are noticeably cooler than Amman.
Use 'As-salamu alaikum' when entering shops or speaking to locals; handshakes are standard with men, but women should wait for the other person to extend their hand first.
Friday afternoons and weekends attract large Jordanian family groups; visit mid-week or in shoulder seasons (spring/fall) for a more contemplative experience.
Hiring an English-speaking local guide from the castle entrance enriches your understanding of Crusader-Ayyubid history and modern village life.

places.name: Downtown Amman
places.description: The historic heart of Amman featuring the Roman Theater, bustling souks, traditional spice markets, and local restaurants serving authentic Levantine cuisine. It's the most authentic and economical way to experience Amman's daily life and culinary traditions.

activities.detail: Explore this 2,000-year-old amphitheater carved into the hillside, still used for performances and events, with views across downtown Amman.
Wander through the fragrant rows of stalls selling cardamom, sumac, za'atar, and other essential Levantine spices in their most authentic form.
Browse the glittering jewelry shops in this traditional gold market where haggling is expected and prices are negotiable.
Climb to this elevated archaeological site overlooking downtown to see Umayyad palace ruins and enjoy panoramic views of the city.
Visit this 1924 mosque with its distinctive green dome, an architectural landmark and symbol of downtown Amman's religious heritage.
Walk through this vibrant pedestrian zone filled with cafes, galleries, and street art, blending traditional and contemporary Amman culture.

food.detail: Crispy deep-fried chickpea fritters served hot in pita bread with tahini and pickled vegetables, a ubiquitous breakfast and street food staple.
A traditional Jordanian lamb dish served in a rich yogurt-based sauce with rice, almonds, and pine nuts, representing local culinary heritage.
Creamy chickpea puree topped with olive oil and paprika, often paired with a roasted eggplant dip, best eaten at a local breakfast restaurant.
Shaved meat cooked on a vertical spit and wrapped in pita with garlic sauce and vegetables, sold by numerous street vendors throughout downtown.
Visit a busy souk juice vendor for freshly pressed pomegranate, orange, or guava juice made from local fruits, thick with pulp and no additives.
A warm cheese pastry soaked in sugar syrup and topped with pistachios, a beloved dessert found in traditional sweets shops near the souks.

culture.detail: In the souks and markets, prices are rarely fixed—expect to negotiate respectfully, especially in the spice and gold markets where it's part of the transaction ritual.
While downtown is relatively liberal, covering shoulders and knees is respectful, particularly near mosques and in more conservative sections of the souk.
Downtown is most bustling and pleasant in the early morning (6–9 AM) when locals shop and eat breakfast, or after 5 PM when it cools and shops reopen.
Shop owners and locals often offer tea or coffee as a gesture of welcome; accepting shows respect and may lead to better prices or genuine local conversation.

places.name: Philadelphia Archaeological Museum
places.description: Located near the Citadel, this museum houses artifacts from the ancient Greek city of Philadelphia (Amman's classical name) and surrounding regions, displaying coins, pottery, and inscriptions. It provides valuable context for understanding how the site evolved through different historical periods.

activities.detail: Study the extensive numismatic collection showcasing Seleucid and Ptolemaic coins that reveal Philadelphia's trade networks and economic history.
Observe well-preserved ceramic vessels and fragments from Hellenistic and Roman periods that illustrate daily life and artistic techniques of ancient residents.
Examine stone inscriptions and carved tablets written in Greek that provide epigraphic evidence of religious practices, commerce, and administration.
Walk to the nearby Citadel of Amman located just above the museum to see the archaeological layers and fortifications of Philadelphia's acropolis.
Inspect marble and limestone sculpture pieces including architectural elements and decorative reliefs from Hellenistic temples and public buildings.
Learn how archaeologists uncovered different occupational layers revealing Philadelphia's settlement from the Iron Age through the Islamic period.

food.text: Hummus at a Traditional Amman Mezze Restaurant
food.detail: Enjoy creamy chickpea puree with tahini and lemon, a staple Levantine dish served warm with olive oil and pine nuts in restaurants near the Citadel.
Try a fresh mixed green salad with tomatoes, cucumbers, radishes, and crispy pieces of fried pita bread dressed with sumac and lemon vinaigrette.
Sample skewered seasoned lamb grilled over charcoal and served with rice, vegetables, and yogurt sauce at local Ammani establishments.
Taste vine leaves filled with spiced rice and sometimes meat, a traditional preparation commonly served cold or warm as an appetizer.
Indulge in the sweet shredded pastry dessert topped with melted cheese and clotted cream, often served warm with honey drizzle.

culture.detail: Wear modest clothing that covers shoulders and knees, particularly when visiting the adjacent Citadel which contains religious buildings and sensitive areas.
Ask museum staff before photographing display cases and inscriptions, as some artifacts may have restrictions or require special permission.
Plan your museum visit for early morning or late afternoon to avoid midday heat, and allow 1–2 hours for a thorough exploration of the collections.
Using simple Arabic phrases like 'Marhaba' (hello) and 'Shukran' (thank you) with museum staff and nearby shopkeepers shows respect and enhances your experience.

places.name: Wadi Zarqa Nature Reserve
places.description: A scenic wadi (valley) with flowing water and lush vegetation located east of Amman, offering hiking trails and bird watching opportunities in a natural landscape. It provides a refreshing escape from urban areas and showcases Jordan's diverse ecosystems.

activities.detail: Follow the primary hiking path along the wadi floor, where you'll walk beside the flowing Zarqa River and experience the lush riparian vegetation and cool water environment.
Visit early morning to observe migratory and resident birds such as kingfishers, herons, and eagles that congregate along the water and cliff faces.
Cool off in natural pools formed by the river flow, particularly at wider sections where the current slows and water is safe for swimming.
Capture panoramic views from elevated vantage points where sandstone cliffs frame the green valley below and the river winds through the landscape.
Investigate the distinctive geological features including canyon walls, natural arches, and layered sedimentary formations that reveal Jordan's geological history.
Set up a meal at designated spots near the riverbank where shade from vegetation and the sound of flowing water create an idyllic natural setting.

food.detail: Fresh-caught trout from the Zarqa River, often prepared grilled or pan-fried at nearby lodges and served with local herbs and lemon.
A traditional Jordanian dish of lamb or chicken cooked in a tangy yogurt sauce and served over rice, commonly prepared in village homestays near the reserve.
Local salads featuring foraged plants and fresh vegetables from the wadi region, dressed with olive oil and sumac, reflecting traditional Bedouin cooking.
Strong, cardamom-scented Arabic coffee served with fresh dates, offered as a welcome tradition by local guides and families in the surrounding villages.
Freshly baked Jordanian flatbread topped with zaatar spice mix and olive oil, available at rest stops and local eateries at the wadi entrance.

culture.detail: The wadi is a sacred natural space for local communities; stay on marked paths, take all trash with you, and avoid disturbing wildlife or vegetation.
Wear conservative clothing appropriate for a rural Jordanian area, with covered shoulders and knees, though hiking attire that is practical and modest is acceptable.
Jordanians value respectful greetings; learn basic Arabic phrases like 'Salam alaikum' (peace be upon you) and accept any offered tea or hospitality graciously.
Plan visits for autumn through spring when temperatures are mild and the river flow is consistent; avoid summer heat and occasional winter flooding.

places.name: Umm Qais
places.description: An ancient Greco-Roman city in northern Jordan overlooking the Jordan Valley, Sea of Galilee, and Golan Heights, with ruins of temples, theaters, and a museum showcasing artifacts. Located about an hour and a half from Amman, it offers historical depth combined with dramatic panoramic vistas.

activities.detail: Walk through the remarkably preserved Roman theatre carved into the hillside, offering insight into ancient entertainment and gatherings.
Browse artifacts including mosaics, pottery, coins, and statuary from the Greco-Roman period housed in the on-site museum.
Trek along the ridge trails to multiple overlooks with sweeping views of the Jordan Valley, Sea of Galilee, and Golan Heights on clear days.
Examine the foundations and remaining columns of an early Byzantine church, reflecting the religious transition of the region.
Capture images of the reconstructed Corinthian columns and ancient architectural fragments arranged across the archaeological site.
Stroll through excavated house foundations and streets to understand daily life in this prosperous Roman border town.

food.detail: Enjoy the Levantine specialty of lamb in yogurt sauce served with thin flatbread, often prepared using local recipes from nearby Bedouin communities.
Taste strained yogurt topped with the region's signature thyme-sumac blend, typically served as an appetizer with fresh pita.

food.text: Grilled fish from the Jordan Valley
food.detail: Sample freshwater fish caught from local sources and simply grilled with lemon and herbs, reflecting the area's proximity to water sources.
Try hand-rolled grape leaves filled with seasoned rice and meat, a traditional preparation common in northern Jordan's rural hospitality.
Share the customary welcome beverage of cardamom-infused coffee paired with fresh or stuffed dates, essential to Jordanian hospitality.

culture.text: Dress modestly and wear sturdy shoes
culture.detail: The site involves extensive walking over uneven ancient stone; lightweight long pants and covered shoulders show respect for the rural Jordanian setting.

culture.text: Visit early morning or late afternoon
culture.detail: Temperatures soar at midday on the exposed hilltop, and morning and golden-hour light enhance both photography and comfort.
A simple greeting in Arabic opens warm interactions with site staff and nearby villagers who appreciate visitors' respect for local customs.

culture.text: Bring plenty of water and sun protection
culture.detail: The archaeological site offers minimal shade; visitors should carry at least 2 liters of water, hat, and sunscreen for safe exploration.

restaurants.name: Petra Kitchen
restaurants.description: A celebrated family-run establishment serving authentic Jordanian home cooking in a warm, intimate setting.

restaurants.name: Hashem
restaurants.description: An iconic institution since 1952 serving simple, exceptional street food in the heart of downtown.
restaurants.mustOrder: Falafel and hummus with fresh pita

restaurants.name: Al-Quds Restaurant
restaurants.description: Popular casual spot on vibrant Rainbow Street offering traditional Palestinian mezze and grilled meats.

restaurants.name: Kan Zaman
restaurants.description: Beautifully restored historical house serving upscale Jordanian cuisine with garden seating and cultural atmosphere.

restaurants.name: Jaya Bistro
restaurants.description: Contemporary upscale bistro blending Middle Eastern and international flavours in a sophisticated setting.

restaurants.name: Sufra by Hadiqat Al-Quds
restaurants.address: Abu Bakr Al-Siddiq Street, Jabal Amman
restaurants.description: Charming restaurant in a heritage building offering traditional Jordanian cuisine with authentic recipes and warm hospitality.

restaurants.name: Reem Al Bawadi
restaurants.description: Casual Emirati chain with authentic Gulf cuisine, known for quality camel meat and fresh seafood preparations.

restaurants.name: Fakhreldin
restaurants.description: Upscale Lebanese fine dining with elegantly presented dishes and an extensive wine list.
restaurants.mustOrder: Grilled sea bass with seasonal herbs

restaurants.name: Dbaldwin Savoie
restaurants.description: High-end French restaurant offering refined European cuisine in an elegant dining environment.

restaurants.name: Baraka Café
restaurants.description: Hip neighbourhood café and restaurant popular with locals, serving creative Middle Eastern dishes in an artistic setting.
restaurants.mustOrder: Roasted vegetable mezze with house bread

restaurants.name: Pasha
restaurants.description: Upscale Turkish restaurant featuring traditional and contemporary Ottoman cuisine with refined presentation.

restaurants.name: Zaza Bazaar
restaurants.description: Relaxed bistro and lounge offering fusion cuisine with Middle Eastern influences and creative cocktails.

restaurants.name: Tannoureen
restaurants.description: Elegant Lebanese restaurant specializing in wood-fired oven preparations and traditional mountain cuisine.
restaurants.mustOrder: Manakish with za'atar from a wood-fired oven

restaurants.name: Maggie's
restaurants.description: Popular international restaurant and bar frequented by expats, serving burgers, steaks, and global comfort food.
restaurants.mustOrder: Premium beef burger with hand-cut fries
